data.title: Health Care Up as Traders Seek Out Evergreen Sector -- Health Care Roundup
data.description:
Health-care companies rose as traders sought out evergreen sectors in light of recent volatility.

Shares of Eli Lilly rose, and the maker of Zepbound and other obesity drugs became the first pharmaceutical company to hit $1 trillion in market capitalization.

British drugmaker GSK and U.S. biotechnology company AnaptysBio are suing one another, with dueling allegations about breaching a licensing agreement for cancer treatment Jemperli.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-21-25 1734ET

data.title: Consumer Cos Up, as Bubble Fears Subside -- Consumer Roundup
data.description:
Consumer companies rallied, almost wiping out losses for the week as fears about interest rates and financial bubbles subsided.

Shoppers across all income levels are weary of higher prices and searching for deals, but purchasing habits have remained stable, BJ's Wholesale Club said.

British Airways' parent company IAG said it aimed to join the bidding process for a stake in Portugal flag-carrier TAP, as it contends with Air France-KLM and Deutsche Lufthansa for a slice of the state-owned carrier with coveted routes bridging Europe and Latin America.

Hong Kong-based conglomerate CK Hutchison is planning a dual listing of AS Watson, one of the world's largest health and beauty retailers, which could raise up to $2 billion.

Consumer sentiment slid in November compared with last month, the University of Michigan's monthly survey found.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-21-25 1733ET

data.title: Financials Up After Comments From Fed's Williams -- Financials Roundup
data.description:
Shares of banks and other financial institutions rose after comments from Federal Reserve Bank of New York President John Williams quelled rate fears.

Some beaten-down, high-risk shares such as those of private-credit investment firm Blue Owl rebounded, as fears about Wall Street after-shocks from a potential artificial-intelligence bubble subsided.

Shares of alternative investment giant Blackstone rallied, trimming losses for the year to date to around 18%.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-21-25 1732ET

data.title: Tech Up in Volatile Trading -- Tech Roundup
data.description:
Shares of technology companies rose in volatile trading as traders moved in and out of beaten-down artificial-intelligence players and fears about a bubble in the sub-sector percolated.

Nvidia shares were more or less flat, giving up gains earlier in the session. Shares of the chip maker, a poster child for the AI boom, fell Thursday, slipping into correction territory despite reporting blockbuster earnings growth.

Shares of Oracle, one of the companies that has borrowed money most aggressively to build out AI infrastructure, lost more than 5% and were down by more than 45% from their recent peak at one stage.

Few Wall Street strategists see an imminent bust in equity or credit markets due to AI speculation, but most are keeping an eye on the warning signs in markets. "We remain watchful of overheating risks, particularly a late 1990s-style decoupling between equities and credit, driven by heavy issuance and releveraging," said credit strategists at brokerage Morgan Stanley, in a note to clients.

Chinese firm Moonshot AI is nearing a dollar funding round that could boost its valuation to around $4 billion.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-21-25 1732ET

data.title: Communications Services Up Amid Deal Activity -- Communications Services Roundup
data.description:
Communications-services companies rallied amid deal activity.

Paramount Skydance, Comcast and Netflix have submitted bids for Warner Bros. Discovery, owner of the storied Warner Bros. movie and television studio and HBO, with Paramount the only bidder seeking to take over the whole company. Shares of all four companies rose. Separately, Paramount won most media rights to show soccer's UEFA Champions League in the U.K. and Germany, securing prized live-sports properties in an auction that raised 10 billion euros, equivalent to $11.53 billion.

Shares of videogame maker Ubisoft Entertainment climbed after a weeklong suspension, as the videogame maker posted higher sales for its fiscal second quarter due to stronger-than-expected partnerships and a robust back catalog.

Nokia is investing an additional $4 billion in U.S.-based manufacturing and research and development in partnership with the Trump administration, as the Finnish telecommunications company looks to optimize its network infrastructure for artificial intelligence.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-21-25 1731ET

data.title: Utilities Up, but Down on Week, As AI Bubble Fears Fade -- Utilities Roundup
data.description:
Shares of power producers ticked up, paring their losses for the week, as AI bubble fears faded.

The usually sleepy utilities sector has The SPDR Select Sector Utilities exchange-traded fund, which tracks the utility industry group of the S&P 500, fell 1% for the week, but remains roughly 16% higher for the year to date.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-21-25 1730ET

California to Sell $52.6 Million of Multifamily Housing Revenue Bonds
data.description:
By Chris Wack


The California Municipal Finance Authority plans to sell $52.6 million of multifamily housing revenue bonds for a rental housing development in Los Angeles.

Proceeds from the Series 2025 bonds are expected to be loaned to 6033 De Soto LP to pay a portion of the costs of buying, constructing and equipping a 207-unit multifamily rental housing development, according to a document posted Tuesday on MuniOS.

The project, known as the Winslow, is located on a 1.47 acre site, and will include 159 parking spaces for resident use only. Common area improvements will include a fitness room, mail room, community room and interior courtyard.

Once the bonds are issued, construction is expected to be completed within 24 months.

Interest on the bonds will be payable starting on June 1, and then the following Dec. 1. Interest rates and yields have not yet been set. The bonds accumulate principle from 2028 through 2059.

The bonds are limited obligations of the authority.

The authority assists with the financing of economic development, public benefit, and charitable activities throughout California by issuing taxable and tax-exempt bonds.

Moody's assigned the bonds a rating of Aa1.

Lument Securities is the lead underwriter.


Write to Chris Wack at chris.wack@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-21-25 1718ET

AstraZeneca unveils USD2 billion manufacturing investment in Maryland
data.description:
(Alliance News) - AstraZeneca PLC on Friday announced plans to invest USD2 billion to expand its long-standing manufacturing footprint in Maryland.

The Cambridge, England-based pharmaceuticals company said this includes a significant expansion of its flagship biologics manufacturing facility in Frederick and construction of a new state-of-the-art facility in Gaithersburg for the development and clinical supply of innovative molecules to be used in clinical trials.

Friday's announcement is part of AstraZeneca’s USD50 billion investment announced in July and follows a series of US commitments over the past six months, including unveiling a new cell therapy manufacturing facility in Rockville, Maryland, a new drug substance manufacturing facility in Virginia and the expansion of the Company's existing manufacturing facility in Coppell, Texas.

Shares in AstraZeneca closed up 0.6% at 13,734.00 pence each in London on Friday.

By Jeremy Cutler, Alliance News reporter

Comments and questions to newsroom@alliancenews.com

Copyright 2025 Alliance News Ltd. All Rights Reserved.

data.title: What's Behind the Selloff in Nvidia Stock?
data.description:
Nvidia (NVDA) had a strong earnings report, but then shares fell as investors keep worrying about an AI bubble. Nvidia, the world's most valuable company, announced Wednesday that sales will be about $65 billion in the January quarter, roughly $3 billion more than analysts predicted. Bloomberg's Carmen Reinicke reports.

Bloomberg videos, provided by MT Newswires

data.title: Pfizer Sells $6 Billion of Notes
data.description:
By Kelly Cloonan


Pfizer sold an aggregate of $6 billion in notes due in the coming years.

The pharmaceutical giant sold $500 million of floating rate notes and $1 billion in 3.9% notes, both due in 2027, according to a filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission on Friday.

Pfizer also sold $1 billion of 4.2% notes due of 2030, $1.25 billion of 4.5% notes due of 2032 and $1.25 billion of 4.9% notes due in 2035. It also sold $500 million each of both 5.6% notes due in 2055 and 5.7% notes due in 2065.

Citigroup, Deutsche Bank, JPMorgan and Mizuho Securities are listed as the offering's underwriters.


Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-21-25 1654ET

US stocks jump on Fed rate cut bets but lose ground on the week
data.description:
NEW YORK (Reuters) -Wall Street stocks closed sharply higher on Friday as rising expectations of a December interest rate cut by the Federal Reserve offset concerns over lofty tech valuations.

A broad rally started gathering momentum by late morning, pushing all three major U.S. stock indexes to substantial gains on the day.

Benchmark Treasury yields fell, the dollar was steady and bitcoin pared its losses.

The volatile session caps a tumultuous week in which U.S. and world stocks lost ground from last Friday's close.

The Fed, deprived of official U.S. economic data during the recently ended government shutdown, at last got a fresh glimpse of the labor market on Thursday, which showed the unemployment rate unexpectedly ticking higher.

As a result, financial markets are pricing in an increased likelihood of a third and final rate cut this year from the Fed. CME's FedWatch tool sets the odds at 73.3%, a significant bump from 39.1% on Thursday.

Messaging from monetary policymakers is mixed. New York Fed President John Williams said the Fed could still cut rates in the near term, while Dallas Fed President Lorie Logan called for them to be left on hold while the central bank assesses the effect of current rates on the economy.

"New York Fed President Williams' comments seem to have shifted the perception on that December rate cut potential," said Ross Mayfield, investment strategy analyst at Baird in Louisville, Kentucky.

"Part of the reason for the move today is that Williams was seen as one of the hawkish leans, so the market could perceive it representing someone stepping over the line towards the dovish point of view."

"Other than that, yesterday was a pretty broad and rough selling day into the close, so (the market was) primed for some bounce," Mayfield added.

Solid earnings from artificial intelligence vanguards, notably chipmaker Nvidia, momentarily eased concerns that AI-related tech stocks, which powered the stock market's rally in recent months, are overpriced and could be due for a correction.

The third-quarter earnings season is nearly wrapping up, with more than 94% of the companies in the S&P 500 having reported. Of those, 83% beat earnings estimates, according to LSEG data.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 493.30 points, or 1.08%, to 46,245.56, the S&P 500 rose 64.20 points, or 0.98%, to 6,602.96 and the Nasdaq Composite rose 195.04 points, or 0.88%, to 22,273.08. 

European stocks ended lower, logging a weekly decline due to worries over stretched tech valuations, while defense shares slid on signs of progress toward ending Russia's war on Ukraine.

MSCI's gauge of stocks across the globe rose 2.73 points, or 0.28%, to 971.26.

The pan-European STOXX 600 index fell 0.33%, while Europe's broad FTSEurofirst 300 index fell 7.27 points, or 0.32%.

Emerging market stocks fell 36.17 points, or 2.64%, to 1,335.37. MSCI's broadest index of Asia-Pacific shares outside Japan closed lower by 2.67%, at 685.82, while Japan's Nikkei fell 1,198.06 points, or 2.40%, to 48,625.88.

The dollar looked set to register a weekly gain but weakened against the yen, as Japanese officials stepped up their verbal intervention to stem the yen's decline.

The dollar index, which measures the greenback against a basket of currencies including the yen and the euro, fell 0.01% to 100.15, with the euro down 0.09% at $1.1517.

Against the Japanese yen, the dollar weakened 0.68% to 156.41.

Cryptocurrencies sank to multi-month lows amid a broader flight from riskier assets. Bitcoin fell 2.93% to $84,661.00. Ethereum declined 4.64% to $2,744.76.

U.S. Treasury yields dipped as Fed rate cut bets rose.

The yield on benchmark U.S. 10-year notes fell 4.1 basis points to 4.063%, from 4.104% late on Thursday. The 30-year bond yield fell 1.7 basis points to 4.715% from 4.732% late on Thursday.

The 2-year note yield, which typically moves in step with interest rate expectations for the Federal Reserve, fell 5.1 basis points to 3.508%, from 3.558% late on Thursday.

Oil prices extended their decline for a third session, touching a one-month low as the U.S. pushed for a Russia-Ukraine peace deal.

U.S. crude fell 1.59% to settle at $58.06 per barrel, while Brent settled at $62.56 per barrel, down 1.29% on the day.

Spot gold fell 0.29% to $4,065.29 an ounce. U.S. gold futures fell 0.05% to $4,054.30 an ounce.

(Reporting by Stephen Culp; Additional reporting by Elizabeth Howcroft; Editing by Mark Potter, Richard Chang and Edmund Klamann)

By Stephen Culp

data.title: Goldman's 2026 Outlook in a Volatile Market
data.description:
Goldman Sachs Asset Management is out with its 2026 outlook and the message is clear: finding catalysts amid complexity. From shifting central bank policies to AI, trade realignments, and geopolitical risks, Goldman sees a dynamic investment backdrop that demands active management and diversification. Greg Calnon, Goldman Sachs Asset Management Global Co-Head of Public Investing, joined Bloomberg Open Interest to talk about the bank's outlook.

Bloomberg videos, provided by MT Newswires
